typical_tension_triggers: Cramped vertical spaces forcing precise jumping
Large creatures (horse, wheel) with momentum-based attacks
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Gradual escalation from ground-based enemies to multi-dimensional hazards (aerial, aquatic); mid-game introduces mobility power-ups to match complexity increase

difficulty_curve.early_game: Basic platforming and ground-level enemies establish foundational movement and collision mechanics
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Introduction of aerial threats (planes, balloons) and mobility power-ups (boots); increased enemy density and behavioral complexity
difficulty_curve.late_game: Aquatic hazards (submarines, water-level variants); large creatures (horses, wheels) with heavy collision weight; multi-threat scenarios requiring power-up management
notable_spikes: First aerial enemy introduction (planes/balloons requiring new spatial awareness)
Water-level sections with submarine enemies and altered momentum physics
Late-game combination encounters featuring both grounded and aerial threats simultaneously

notable_level_patterns.description: Game progression introduces enemy varieties in distinct phases: ground (basic) → aerial (planes, balloons) → aquatic (submarines) → heavy/large (horses, wheels). Each introduces new spatial demands and evasion patterns.

Boots and star power-ups appear mid-game coinciding with introduction of more complex enemy patterns, suggesting intentional difficulty scaling via mechanical enablement rather than pure enemy tuning.
Early levels constrain threats to horizontal plane (ground enemies); mid-game expands vertically (aerial); late-game adds depth dimension (aquatic). Mirrors classic NES design progression.

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Puss n Boots: Pero's Great Adventure)
